attributes.stem:
A 63-year-old woman presents to the Pre-admission Clinic for assessment before an elective transphenoidal resection of a pituitary macroadenoma compressing the optic chiasm. She has a medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and a non-ST elevation myocardial infarction five years ago that was managed medically. Her regular medications are aspirin, metformin, empagliflozin, metoprolol, and perindopril. She does not smoke and lives independently.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 28 kg/m2.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following medications should be continued throughout the peri-operative period?
attributes.explanation:
This patient requires preoperative medication optimisation before high-risk neurosurgery. The key consideration is balancing the risks of continuing versus stopping each medication.

Metoprolol should be continued through the perioperative period. Abrupt cessation of beta blockers can precipitate rebound tachycardia and hypertension, which could be catastrophic during surgery. Beta blockers also provide cardioprotection in the perioperative period.

Aspirin should be stopped 5-7 days before this procedure. While aspirin reduces thrombotic risk, this surgery carries a high risk of bleeding. Given her myocardial infarction was five years ago and managed medically, her thrombotic risk is relatively low.

Empagliflozin, a sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT-2) inhibitor, should be stopped 3 days before surgery to prevent euglycaemic ketoacidosis, which can occur even with normal blood glucose levels.

Metformin should be stopped 24-48 hours before surgery due to the risk of lactic acidosis if acute kidney injury occurs. Alternative glucose control with insulin may be required.

Perindopril should be withheld on the morning of surgery as angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors can cause significant hypotension during anaesthesia, particularly during induction.

Postoperatively, these medications can be restarted according to the patient's clinical condition, with particular attention to blood pressure control and glycaemic management.
learningPoints: Beta blockers must be continued perioperatively to prevent rebound tachycardia and hypertension
Aspirin can be stopped 5-7 days before high-risk bleeding procedures if thrombotic risk is low
SGLT-2 inhibitors should be stopped 3 days before surgery to prevent euglycaemic ketoacidosis
ACE inhibitors can be held on the morning of surgery to reduce intraoperative hypotension
references: Patel P, Whinney C. Perioperative Medication Management. Med Clin North Am. 2024;108(6):1135-1153. doi:10.1016/j.mcna.2024.05.002
Binkley, 2020. Beta Blockers [online]. Cardiology Advisor. Access at: https://www.thecardiologyadvisor.com/home/decision-support-in-medicine/cardiology/beta-blockers/.

Australian Medicines Handbook, 2020. Metoprolol [online]. AMH. Access at: https://amhonline-amh-net-au.proxy.library.adelaide.edu.au/chapters/cardiovascular-drugs/antihypertensives/beta-blockers/metoprolol

Australian Diabetes Society, 2011. Peri-operative diabetes management guidelines. Diabetes Society. Access at: https://diabetessociety.com.au/downloads/ADS%20Diabetes%20and%20Surgery%20Guidelines%20DRAFT%20May%202011.pdf.
